summary: Atwood observes that SourceForge's top downloads are dominated by P2P file sharing clients and media ripping tools, illustrating that users are primarily motivated by getting free content rather than free (as in freedom) software. He notes the irony that users will accept significant security risks—including malware-laden executables—to access pirated content. The post is a brief but pointed commentary on user behavior and priorities.
key_points: Seven of ten top SourceForge downloads are P2P file sharing clients, with the remaining three being media ripping tools
Users prioritize 'free as in beer' (no cost) over 'free as in speech' (freedom/open source ideals)
Users willingly expose themselves to security risks, with half of shared executables containing malicious code
Even running as non-Administrator won't protect users from this malware exposure
money_quotes: Seven of the ten top downloads are pure p2p file sharing clients. The other three directly relate to media ripping.
Somehow I doubt freedom of speech is high on most users' priority lists.
Users will jump through any hoops to get things for free as in beer.
key_insight: When it comes to software, most users care about 'free as in beer' (zero cost) far more than 'free as in speech' (open source freedom), and will accept enormous security risks to get content for nothing.
